assessment_items.question_content:
**Originally the Colossus of Constantine was probably 40 feet high and stood . . .**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/279c0f57e40b2a2bf63692a4ad0d9543.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Originally, the statue was probably 40 feet high and stood in a niche in the Basilica of Maxentius and Constantine, a large public building in the Forum.**

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-rome/v/colossus-of-constantine)**

choices.content: in a niche in the Basilica of Maxentius and Constantine.
next to the Column of Trajan.
in a niche in the Pantheon.
in front of the Arch of Constantine.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Constantine moved the administrative center of the empire from Rome to [[☃ dropdown 1]], which contributed to the decline of Rome as a city.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/279c0f57e40b2a2bf63692a4ad0d9543.png)
hints:
**Constantine moved the administrative center of the empire from Rome to Constantinople, which contributed to the decline of Rome as a city.**

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-rome/v/colossus-of-constantine)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Portraits of Roman emperors were entirely realistic.**


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/036e01a651145a77467c589cd068e7c2.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Portraits of Roman emperors often included elements of realism and idealism.** 

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-rome/v/colossus-of-constantine)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**The emerging abstraction that is present in the Colossus of Constantine came to be associated with . . .**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/036e01a651145a77467c589cd068e7c2.png)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**This sense of abstraction may have had a broader appeal than the more classical styles of the past and came to be associated with the rise of Christianity.**

**[Watch this video to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-c-e--ancient-cultures-1/ancient-rome/v/colossus-of-constantine)**

choices.content: the Sack of Rome in 410 C.E.
